behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: A mock LLM can be configured to always return context-aware responses, hiding the real failure: that the actual LLM produces generic output or ignores the email context. The prompt quality and LLM behavior determine whether references are specific or generic. Mocking this dependency tests the mock, not the system.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM-based draft generator with a multi-point email (asking about timeline, budget, and stakeholders). Verify the generated draft explicitly references at least two specific points from the email by semantic similarity search or manual inspection. Run this test multiple times to account for LLM non-determinism.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: Detecting factual contradictions likely requires semantic understanding of both the draft and KB entries. If the checker uses an LLM or learned semantic model for contradiction detection, mocking the model will always pass (the mock can be designed to return contradictions). The real failure is that the actual semantic understanding fails to catch subtle contradictions (e.g., 'mid-2020s' contradicting 'founded 2023'). If implementation is purely rule-based matching, Integration could work, but semantic checking is likely.
behaviors.test_description: Create a draft with semantic contradictions: 'Our product reached profitability within 2 years' against KB entry 'Profitability: Year 4'. Run the real consistency checker. Verify it flags this contradiction (and similar ones with rephrasing) consistently. If the checker uses an LLM for semantic analysis, use real LLM calls; avoid mocking the semantic layer.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: The LLM may ignore the instruction to include a disclaimer and fabricate facts anyway. Mocking the LLM to always return a disclaimer hides the real failure: that the actual LLM generates unsupported claims even when prompted to disclaim. This is purely a question of whether the real LLM follows its instructions under no-KB conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a KB with entries unrelated to the email thread topic. Generate a draft with KB access disabled or filtered to return no results. Verify the draft explicitly contains a disclaimer (e.g., 'This is my best assessment, but I don't have verified sources for...'). Run with the real LLM to catch cases where it ignores the instruction.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the workflow logic itself fails to block sending. While a Unit test with mocked approval could catch missing approval checks, Integration testing with the real workflow object is needed to catch state bugs, race conditions, or workflow initialization issues. You need the actual ApprovalWorkflow object, but mocking the email send operation is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real ApprovalWorkflow, set a draft, request approval but do not grant it, attempt to send, and verify a blocking error is raised. Then approve and verify sending succeeds. Use real workflow objects to catch control-flow bugs, but mock the email send API.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: This behavior spans multiple agentic steps in sequence (draft generation and fact checking both depend on real LLM outputs). The failure could be in integration between steps (e.g., the draft format breaks the fact checker, or the fact checker output is not presented correctly), not in individual steps. Mocking the LLMs at intermediate steps would hide failures in how their outputs are passed and consumed downstream. Testing with real LLM calls for both generation and checking is needed.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full pipeline with a real email thread: (1) read and parse the thread, (2) call the real LLM draft generator, (3) run the real fact checker on the draft, (4) present the result for approval. Verify that all steps complete without data format errors, each step's output is compatible with the next step's input, and the approval UI receives all necessary information. Use real LLM calls for generation and fact-checking.
